children.text: Introducing an Open Source Medium-like Editor
Users familiar with Medium will find this editor instantly recognizable; it closely replicates the look and feel of
while introducing powerful additional features that we will now examine in detail.

Triggered by text highlighting, the inline toolbar provides options to format and convert text. Its appearance, however, is conditional; it does not trigger, for instance, when the selection includes a math node or is located within a code block.
The block toolbar appears when the selection is collapsed at the beginning of a paragraph.

There are three types of headings: title, main, and subheadings. To convert text into a heading, select the desired text and use the heading toggle in the inline toolbar. Toggling it once converts the text into a main heading, while toggling it a second time converts it into a subheading. A special case exists for the title heading: if a sentence at the very beginning of the document is converted into a main heading, it automatically becomes the title heading. This behavior ensures that there is only one title heading per document, and it is always located at the document's start.
paragraph immediately following a heading will automatically adjust its vertical position to be closer to the heading.

provides options for both block and inline quotes, this editor is limited to inline quotes. To apply an inline quote, select the text and use the corresponding toggle in the inline toolbar. Below is an example:
What would this look like if it were easy? - Tim Ferriss
The editor supports LaTeX rendering using the newly released MathJax v4. Create inline math by enclosing expressions within
. Double click on a rendered math node to edit it as text; moving the selection away will render it back into a math node. Below is an example.
Note that if you start a new paragraph with `$$ `, a math highlight block node will be automatically created for you.
Some of my favourite features of MathJax v4 are custom font support and automatic line break insertion. Note that these have to explicitly configured in the
Future plans also include the addition of LaTeX validation functionality to alert users to invalid expressions.
To insert a code block, use the block toolbar. Upon entering a code block, a language selection menu will become available in the top right corner.
// Copy the result to the clipboard using clipboardy
You can also create a code block by starting a new paragraph with "``` "

Please note that currently, only a default theme is supported, and language highlighting is limited to popular languages. The process for adding new languages is straightforward, and documentation explaining how to do this is forthcoming. Support for multiple themes is also planned for a future update.

Adding images is also straightforward. You can either click the image icon on the block toolbar and select the image you wish to insert, or simply paste a copied image directly. Note that GIFs are also supported
The editor supports ordered, unordered, and nested lists. To create an ordered list, begin a paragraph with
. For an unordered list, begin a paragraph with
. While inside a list item, press
to outdent. Currently, the maximum nesting depth is set to 3; however, this limit can be easily adjusted in
As an example, we'll try to prove an inequality.
The proof below follows the style of Leslie Lamport's paper, "How to write a proof."
